title: ArrayList - a list that grows
author: Prodipta Acharjee
concept: An ArrayList is a resizable list: add() puts an item on the end, get(i) reads one, size() counts. It holds objects, not raw primitives.
explanation: When you don't know how many things you'll have, an ArrayList grows as you go. add() appends, get(i) reads by index, size() tells you how many - no fixed length to plan around. One wrinkle: it stores objects, so a list of numbers is ArrayList<Integer>, not int; Java quietly boxes the primitives for you. It's the everyday collection you reach for in Java.

exercises.question: What does this print?
hints: size() counts the items; get(0) is the first.

exercises.question: Which method adds an item to the end of an ArrayList?
Why is it ArrayList<Integer> and not ArrayList<int>?
options: an ArrayList holds objects, not primitives
hints: Integer is the object wrapper for int.
